sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE].
Good afternoon.
My name is Marion Gottesman.
No, my maiden name was [INAUDIBLE] Silberberg.
My current--
Is Gottesman, is G-O-T-T-E-S-M-A-N.
And my maiden name was S-I-L-B-E-R-B-E-R-G, Silberberg.
I was born in Vlotho in Northern part of Germany.
V-L-O-T-H-O.
It's between Bielefeld and Hannover.
Well, I was-- two days ago, I was 69.
Thank you.
It's a very small town.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember too good of things from-- even from before the war, because I was six years old when I started school in 1933.
And we had a very, very tough time, from the beginning.
Well, like I said before I started school in 193.
And a half a year, a year later, we had to sit in a Jew [INAUDIBLE].
On a bench only for Jewish children, in front of all the others.
And there was a lot of anti-Semitism in the northern part of Germany.
So we felt it from the time we were very little.
Yes, yes.
The earlier-- yeah, I have memories from my family, and from my grandmother, which I was very close with.
My grandmother's name was Rebekka Silberberg.
And she came from Krefeld, which is in Rhineland, and got married to her own cousin, first cousin, Israel Silberberg, which I don't remember.
He died in 1926.
And they lived in Vlotho.
And my grandmother-- we lived with my grandmother for a while, in the end of-- before we got arrested.
And I don't know.
It's, I guess, a normal childhood with a few Jewish people.
My grandmother was a big, huge woman, very lovable, and always feeding us.
Yes, very good.
Offhand, I really don't know.
I just know that she was a very good cook.
And she always was interested that nobody should get hungry.
Yeah, very close.
In those times, grandmothers were grandmothers.
There weren't people who did things with you outside of the house.
Let's put it that way.
And she just was a level-- a very lovable person.
Not like here.
You become a grandmother in America, you run around and do all the things you do when you're young, right?
But over there, it's like-- I guess it was like a normal way of grandparents, or grandmothers at the time, being the motherly types for the whole families.
My mother's name is Henny, Henny Silberberg.
And her maiden name was Strauss.
S-T-R-A-U-S-S.
My mother was a beautiful woman.
She was very compassionate.
She always tried to help people.
And her profession was a nurse.
No, this was before she got married that she was a nurse.
She was at the university in Giessen, which is near Frankfurt-- yes, nurse there.
And my father was a very-- I don't know how to explain to-- he wasn't only my father.
He was my friend.
He was everything to me.
And I was everything to him, that I know.
Willy, Willy Silberberg.
Yeah.
I was very close.
I was an only child.
I was the daughter he wanted.
But I also was the son he wanted.
He make me grew up like a tough little kid.
A lot of bicycle riding.
We didn't have a car.
So we went from one town to the other, visiting friends and people he had business to do with.
And, yes, it was my father.
No.
No, not really, not really.
Oh, many times.
There was a boy I grew up with in the town, a Jewish boy.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the trouble we got into is that we come from a small little hilly town.
And we found, naturally, the highest little mountain there.
We would climb up there in dangerous positions, and things like this, and just got-- you know, like little troubles.
They were glad we came home in one piece.
When I was a child-- now, it became a bigger town, because they incorporated a lot of small towns.
It was a small town with 5,000 people living there.
And what I remember, there were never more than 20 Jewish families in town.
And little by little in the beginning, some people moved, and immigrated to the states, or somewhere else.
So by the time that I really remember, there were maybe 10 families left, at the most.
Yes, I had this one boy.
His name was Gunter Katz.
And he was survived also Auschwitz, et cetera.
And he never came back to Vlotho.
He was sent from Vlotho to Holland.
From Holland, he came to Theresienstadt, from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz.
And when he was liberated, I don't know where he was liberated, the Russians took him to Odessa, and flew him back to Holland.
And from in Holland, he found out that none of his family except one aunt survived.
And she was in Belgium.
So she went to-- he went to Brussels to meet her there, and then came to the United States.
But he passed away here.
Yes, yes, yes.
Well, he got in touch with us.
I don't know how come he got our address.
I think he found out through one of the organizations that my mother and I were the only survivors who came back from Vlotho.
And I believe that he got in touch with us already in 1946.
We came here in '47.
So we knew that he was in Brussels.
And so we had each other's address.
And we knew when he would come here.
The other Jewish children I was with, nobody survived.
We were very close.
It was a close little town friendships there.
And the first transport went to [INAUDIBLE].
So nobody came back from there.
Then a transport went to Riga.
They got killed there.
And, actually, the only ones who came back to the town was my mother and me.
We were about five, six children there, not more than that.
And there were different ages, you know.
Yeah, it was a small town, you know.
You couldn't help it.
You're close.
I remember some non-Jewish friends.
But they didn't last long, because even if they wanted to be friendly, they weren't permitted.
They couldn't.
We built our little cars.
We didn't have toys like nowadays.
So we build just little cars and stuff like that.
And--
We actually took wood, and put, like-- I don't know what you call those little cars the kid pull and put things on?
They have them here too, but--
Yeah, like a wagon.
That's what I meant with a car.
We did things like that, and got little wheels from somewhere, and et cetera.
No, not really, I mean, you know, like, I guess what every kid plays.
I don't know.
I really don't know.
We didn't have an opportunity to be active.
What I did is with all the boys there, I played soccer as a kid.
I was the only girl left, because everybody else had left already.
And the others were a little older.
They went to different schools out of that town.
So, you know, it was always with the boys.
I wasn't the best student.
That's for sure.
I didn't like school, because, you know, like, we had one teacher who was very nice to us, who actually, himself, got into trouble because of us Jewish children.
The other teachers were very bad at that particular time already.
And we were glad when we got to school, and got out of school, and they didn't molest us outside, the kids, et cetera.
And we were glad to get home.
School was, like, not very pleasant those years.
All the schooling I have in Germany, really, was from 1933 to 1938.
And then after Kristallnacht, nobody could go to school anymore.
And then they had, in another town-- but by that time we were only, like, maybe three or four children left, I went into another town to school.
And the others went to Holland.
And two of the children who went to Holland, matter of fact, they found out from relatives in Holland that their parents were in a transport to Riga.
And they went over the back to Germany to go with their parents.
So it's, like, not the best times to remember before the war either.
My best subject, art.
I think art, drawing, art, and literature, things like that, things I was interested in.
It wasn't mathematics.
That's for sure.
Mathematics.
But I believe it has something to do with the teacher we had, because for each subject we had different teachers.
And that was the worst Nazi anybody could come across.
So I think that had a lot to do with it, that I was afraid of him.
And I didn't, you know-- that mathematics was something to be afraid of.
Yeah.
This was shortly after the school started.
I started in '33.
So in '33, '34 already, at that particular town.
I would think about 25, 30 children, maybe less.
I really don't know.
We were two in our class.
Right.
Yes, that was this boy I grew up with, and who I met here again.
We were sitting in front of everybody.
And it was called a Juden [INAUDIBLE].
Oh yes, we-- my school memories are not the fondest and the nicest, believe me.
We couldn't go for gym.
We couldn't go for sports, in school.
Because we were Jews.
When they had their gym lessons, we had to either go out of the school and wait outside.
Or the town was so little, for one hour, we would go home, and then go back.
And the same thing was true in musics too, because they had all the Hitler songs, and et cetera.
So we were not-- naturally, not interested, but also not permitted to go for music.
Religion they had in school.
We had to go out.
So it was very little schooling, even in those years already.
No.
We had one very nice teacher.
Yes.
I don't know his first name, you know, Mr.
Absmayer.
German and geography, I believe, literature, things like that.
I tell you something.
He tried-- when the German children started up with us, so he would take them on the side.
He would take our side, leave them alone, this and that.
So he got himself really into trouble already.
Yes, he was very protective of us.
I did meet him after the war when I came back to Germany.
Well, I knew where he was.
So I went to see him, you know.
And he was naturally very glad that I came back, the only one.
Oh yeah, yeah.

I believe he was a teacher of my father in his last years of school, too, because in Europe it was that one teacher was there for so many years that even the parents went to school with them already, you know, on that order.
Yeah.
The mathematics teacher, not only did he hit us.
But he also-- like, when a teacher calls you, let's say to the blackboard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he wouldn't call us by our name.
He would, Jew girl, or Jew boy, et cetera.
So, naturally, that didn't leave a very good thing in us.
He hit us.
He did a lot-- they did a lot of things to us.
And I rather don't remember them.
No, he hit-- no, some of the children were hit.
This was a typical European, I guess, in schools to be hit.
But he had it out for Gunter and me.
That's for sure.
Oh, definitely, any excuse, any excuse.
He didn't need an excuse to do it.
No, he called us the-- very bad names, and things like that, right in front of the children.
They fell right into it, because that's what they were taught in the Hitlerjugend, et cetera.
So these are the memories of Vlotho schools.
We were-- well, everything was in walking distance.
But we walked about maybe 20 minutes, 15 to 20 minutes, to go to school.
With Gunter.
Yeah, they lived a few blocks away from us.
And went together, and his brother, who was a year older, and was in another class.
And that was it.
His brother didn't survive.
He went to Auschwitz and didn't come back.
They were bothering us.
They were calling us names, and things like that.
And even some of the parents of the children did that also.
That was Germany in those times.
Many times, many times when we were kids.
We got beaten up in the streets, and things like that.
But it was a way of surviving and living then already.
They were terrible upset, because they couldn't do anything.
I remember that, at one time, I got into a fight with three boys on the street.
Three against me.
And I was always taller and bigger than Gunter.
So when they started in with him, I [INAUDIBLE] at the time too.
And from one of the boys, the father was a friend, still, at that particular time, of my father.
So he came to-- he took me.
He must have seen the whole thing.
I was, like seven years, eight years old.
He took me and brought me home.
But his son was the one who beat me up.
So this was like one of those things.
Well, sometimes we got beaten up, bloodied, got blue marks, got away with it.
We were little.
Yeah, I was a fighter, all the time.
He would have been a fighter if he was bigger.
He was-- matter of fact, he was born in 1926.
So he was a year older than me.
And he was so tiny at the time when school started for him, which would have been a year before, that they didn't take him.
Not because of that he was Jewish, that he physically was not fully developed as a six-year-old one should be.
So that's how come he started school with me.
And then he became a very strong man later on, one of those things.
German.
No, no.
No, my parents spoke a little French.
They had friends from France who lived, at that particular time, in Germany.
But the only thing I really heard was German, no Yiddish.
We celebrated the holidays.
I'm not from a religious background.
But we had a synagogue.
And had-- I don't know if it was a cantor or a rabbi.
We called him rabbi.
And for the holidays, we would go to shul.
Yes, we had Mr.
Heller, Max Heller.
And he left just before the war and came here to the states.
And then later, we had somebody, Mr.
Lazarus, I believe was his name, yeah, Lazarus.
No, just was a synagogue in Vlotho, Vlotho synagogue.
I don't know.
If it had a name, I don't remember.
Yeah, yes, as a matter of fact, it was quite a big synagogue for a small little place like that.
There was so few Jews living there.
But in that area, were a lot of resorts.
And in those resort areas, just happened not to be many Jewish people living there.
So they came for the holidays to Vlotho.
My favorite Jewish holiday?
Passover.
Because-- because there was wine on the table.
But as children, we didn't get any wine.
So my mother made some kind of a fruit juice at the time, and told me that that was mine.
And, usually, Gunter and his brother came over too.
And we had Passover together, and with his parents, naturally.
We were thinking that we were drinking wine, and always thought we were [YIDDISH], which we were not.
Being drunk, that much Jewish I know-- knew then already.
I mean, we had Jewish words which we used, I think, I remember, but not that we spoke really Yiddish.
No, we had a river.
We had a river.
And as little children, we would go swimming there.
But that later on, we couldn't do that anymore either.
So there were not many things we could do.
No, we were not allowed to go.
By the Germans, by the town, by the burgomeister.
I think we'd maybe have gone swimming till 1937, '38.
But after that, it was nothing.
It was 1936 maybe.
It was very hard.
It was very, very hard.
It was a movie which we could not attend to.
There was a big sign up that Jews couldn't-- were not allowed to go in.
And I remember my mother taking me to another town to see a movie.
And in the middle of one of the movies, it got dark.
They turned off the screen, and announced that now that there are Jews in the movie house, and they should leave.
So we had to leave in the middle of the performance.
That must have been about-- I think it was maybe 1936, '37 already, because after '38, we couldn't even leave the town anymore.
So it must have been in 1936, '37.
No, there were-- we were in a different town.
So there were different Jewish people there.
So everybody went out.
That was way of life.
Because the towns are so small that every-- you know, and so near each other, that everybody knew.
They knew.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, I was a child.
I did what my mother told me to.
I walked out with her.
And I remember meeting other people there.
And my mother acknowledged them, and they acknowledged us.
And then we took, I think, the train back home.
That was it.
I'm trying to.
I think the English version would be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'Curly Head,' something like that, a child with curls.
And I think the actress was a child actress something, like Shirley-- what was her name?
Shirley Temple was, at the same time-- approximately at the same time, right?
[NON-ENGLISH], that I remember.
And I think it must have wanted to see that movie, that maybe I read the book, or something like that.
That was the movie.
I remember.
That's the last time I went to the movie there.
No, no, I never did.
I never did.
One of those.
Well, I remember three homes.
The home where I was born, which was a very huge, large apartment, very modern furniture-- you know, the Bauhaus type what they had in Europe at that time already in the '20s.
And we moved from there.
[INAUDIBLE], 36.
[INAUDIBLE] Street, 36.
I must have been about seven or eight years old, because we had to leave that particular apartment.
It wasn't my parents' house.
We had an apartment there.
And, as a matter of fact, that Gunter, and his mother and father, and his brother, also lived in the same building.
And they had to move.
Why?
Because we were Jews.
That started very early there.
And we moved to a different area in that small town, like from one end to the other town.
And-- but very close to each other.
They had, like, an apartment few blocks-- not even few-- a few houses down from where we lived.
And it was a smaller apartment.
But, you know, when you're a child, you're getting-- you don't know any better.
We had our-- still our same furniture and everything.
And from there--
Let's see, from 1936-37, around that time-- '36 till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1930-- end of 1937.
Maybe a year and a half, two years, something like that.
I don't really remember the numbers of anything.
And then we moved to my grandmother's house.
OK, fine.
